skillName: Recognise the place value of each digit in whole numbers up to millions
misconceptionSummary: Students read the face value of a digit rather than its positional value, especially in multi-digit numbers.

lines.text: This is the face value — not the place value

steps.narration: Here's the mistake. The student sees a five and writes five. But that five isn't sitting in the ones column — so it is not worth five.

visuals.subText: Face value = the digit itself. Place value = digit × column value.

steps.narration: There are two different things here — what the digit looks like, and what it is actually worth. They are only the same when the digit is in the ones column.
Count from the right — the five is in the fifth position, the ten-thousands column. Multiply by ten thousand and you get fifty thousand. That's its true value.

visuals.label: Always find the column first, then multiply

steps.narration: Fifty thousand, not five. The column it sits in is what makes the difference. Find the column first — every time.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: writing the digit itself as the answer (face value) instead of multiplying by the column value (place value).
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: If you find yourself just copying the digit as your answer, stop — you've given the face value, not the place value. Find the column first.
